title: John Harbaugh is betting he can fix Giants bust: ‘Is it too late?’
description: Can John Harbaugh turn this Giants bust into a key player?
fullText:
Four years ago on a windy late-April evening in Las Vegas, Evan Neal walked across the NFL Draft stage, proudly raised his right hand and smiled. He spread his massive arms wide and embraced commissioner Roger Goodell, patting him hard on the back.

A few minutes later, in the backstage area, Neal ducked through a doorway and greeted his family. Impossible to miss, he towered over a small, adoring group —6-foot-7 and 340 pounds, with those 34-inch arms and 10-inch hands.

The Giants dreamed of Neal — universally considered an elite prospect out of Alabama — anchoring their long-struggling offensive line at right tackle for years to come, a perfect bookend to left tackle Andrew Thomas.

But over the next four seasons, Neal faded into invisibility. He struggled to block, couldn’t stay healthy, ripped Giants fans who booed him and lost his starting job in 2024. In 2025, he moved to guard and did not play a single snap that season.

Then, out of nowhere, the Giants re-signed Neal on March 11, instead of letting him leave in free agency. Though Neal got a bare-minimum one-year contract, it was one of this offseason’s most shocking moves.

So can new Giants coach John Harbaugh and his staff fix Neal? Might he suddenly become a productive starter for a team that desperately needs answers at right guard to protect second-year quarterback Jaxson Dart? Or will Harbaugh end up cutting Neal at the conclusion of training camp?

NFL analysts, including former offensive linemen, told NJ.com it will take a lot more than Neal just looking the part of a dominant lineman. That’s never been an issue. Neal’s more significant hurdles, the ex-linemen said, involve sharper technique, quicker processing and rebuilt confidence.

And now, the clock is ticking for a No. 7 overall draft pick who spent the past four years looking like he “couldn’t play dead in a Western,” as former Pro Bowl guard Mark Schlereth said last season.

“You know what we used to call those guys?” Schlereth asked. “First-off-the-bus guys. Send him first off the bus in a tight T-shirt and shorts to intimidate the other team. But don’t put him in the game — because he can’t play.”

This week, Schlereth sounded slightly more hopeful about Harbaugh’s chances of getting something — anything — from Neal in 2026.

“I’ll never say never,” said Schlereth, now a Fox Sports analyst. “We’ve seen some great reclamation projects — just getting into the right system and having somebody believe in you and help fix what ails you. That’s something that could happen.”

For Giants fans, that evening in Las Vegas — and the enthusiasm surrounding Neal — feels like a lifetime ago. But maybe Harbaugh, already cast as the Giants’ savior, will salvage a physically imposing 25-year-old player and make everyone notice him again.

Neal’s previous Giants position coaches — Bobby Johnson for his first two years and Carmen Bricillo for the next two — couldn’t get any legitimate production from him. Why might Mike Bloomgren have better luck in 2026?

Because sometimes, all it takes is a new voice in your ear, said former Jets offensive lineman Damien Woody, now an ESPN analyst. Woody didn’t single out Johnson or Bricillo, but he also didn’t hesitate when asked why Neal has sputtered.

“Listen, when it comes to offensive line play, there’s some bad coaching out there, as far as developing these guys,” Woody said. “I think it’s on everybody [with Neal]. It’s on the player. It’s on the coaching staff. Does the previous coaching staff bear some responsibility? Absolutely. Does the player bear some responsibility? Absolutely.”

Through 29 NFL games and 27 starts, Neal has allowed 98 pressures. Since he entered the league, his pass-blocking win rate (82.9%) ranks last among all tackles, according to ESPN.

On top of that — and frequent durability woes — Neal enraged Giants fans by lashing out at them during the 2023 season.

“Why would a lion concern himself with the opinion of a sheep?” he told NJ.com. “The person that’s commenting on my performance, what does he do? Flip hot dogs and hamburgers somewhere?”

Ultimately, Neal could have made everyone mostly forget that rant by just playing better. He didn’t.

“From a technical standpoint, footwork has been sloppy for him,” Woody said. “He has talent. He’s agile. He’s not a heavy-footed guy. But his footwork and the technical aspect of his game need to be refined a lot. When you’re a player that has talent, you’re going to get multiple opportunities. That’s just the reality of football.”

As Neal returns to the Giants on a one-year, $1.215 million deal that includes no guaranteed money — the cheapest possible contract a player of his experience level can sign — the NFL offensive line world watches with great interest, if only because of his enviable physical traits.

“You look at these dudes, and you’ll be like, ‘Oh my goodness. If I had your size back in the day, I would have been a killer. They would have outlawed me,’” said former Giants left tackle Lomas Brown, who starred in the NFL for 18 years. “But man, some of these guys are like shopping carts. You can just push them around like a shopping cart.”

Schlereth wonders if Neal’s technical issues stem from the most important skill an NFL offensive lineman must master.

“I don’t know if it’s that he just doesn’t process really quickly,” Schlereth said. “What it comes down to is how quickly you can take information in through your eyes and make a snap-second decision on how you’re supposed to block. All these things have to fire in your mind instantly.

“If you can’t process it, that has nothing to do with intelligence. It’s just a God-given ability. Guys who can’t process cannot play in the National Football League. There’s got to be something [with Neal], because it’s not a lack of physicality and athleticism. That dude is blessed.”

During Schlereth’s playing days, he always leaned on this mantra: First contact wins.

To burst out of his stance first — and pop that defensive lineman — Schlereth understood he could not even be “a half tick late” with recognition and processing. Because a half tick late meant his usually sound technique would “fall apart” as he scrambled to recover.

“It’s paralysis by analysis,” Schlereth said.

For a young player like Neal, that triggers a brutal spiral — slow processing, sloppy technique, crumbling confidence.

“He was overmatched out there at tackle,” Schlereth said. “He got spun like a top a bunch, and his technique just would fall apart. Once that happens, confidence falls apart. I’ve been there, man. I’ve been in that position where I had zero confidence, like: ‘Oh my gosh. I can’t block anybody right now.’”

Leonard Davis stormed across the room, eyes fixed on his target.

“I might get my ass kicked right here,” Schlereth thought.

He tried to remember if he had ripped Davis — a disappointing Cardinals left tackle — on TV while working one of his games.

Davis, the second overall pick in 2001, stopped in front of Schlereth.

“OK,” Davis said, as Schlereth nervously waited. “If you’re me, what would you do?”

A few seconds later, Schlereth realized Davis was talking about his upcoming free agency — and a potential move to guard. Schlereth exhaled and chuckled to himself. He wouldn’t get pummeled at this pre-Super Bowl event in 2007 after all.

Then Schlereth offered advice — which applies now to Neal — as Davis listened.

“Leonard, as an NFL tackle, you’re just too big — and you don’t move as well as you need to,” Schlereth said. “If I were you, I would tell whoever [in free agency]: ‘I’m moving inside. That’s what I want to do.’”

Davis did it. He signed with the Cowboys and played right guard, where his 6-foot-6, 355-pound body proved a better fit. Davis immediately made three straight Pro Bowls.

At this point, that seems a lofty goal for Neal. Davis was a much more established player in 2007, with 91 starts. But the move from tackle to guard transformed his career.

Still, the tackle-to-guard adjustment is not simple, even though Neal started at left guard as a college freshman in 2019. Last year during training camp, Neal got beat out for the right guard job by 35-year-old Greg Van Roten, now an unsigned free agent.

Yes, a guard doesn’t have to play on a so-called island and does get more blocking help from the center and tackle. But challenges remain, even for the best linemen.

Brown dominated so often at the University of Florida that the Lions drafted him sixth overall in 1985. But when he tried to play guard during college, he lasted one week.

“I had to get out of there, because everything happens so fast in there,” Brown said. “The d-tackle is right on top of you.”

Most struggling or aging linemen move from tackle to guard. Woody did the opposite for the final four years of his career, and he finished as the Jets’ right tackle from 2008-10. He found guard more difficult because of the “phone booth” tight-quarters nature of the position.

“And the quickest way to the quarterback is where?” he said. “The inside. So that can be an adjustment. Now, you’re starting to see teams put smaller [defensive] guys on the inside to try to create [speed] mismatches with guards. Just because you’re getting kicked inside to guard, it doesn’t mean it’s easier. I tend to think it’s a little harder.”

For all of the shock surrounding Neal’s re-signing, the reality remains: The Harbaugh-led Giants made essentially zero commitment to him, given the nature of his contract.

Strictly from a risk-reward standpoint, giving him one more shot this summer — or even on the practice squad during the 2026 season — makes sense, despite the optics that the team is doubling down on a failed draft pick.

“I don’t mind buying low on an offensive lineman,” said Steve Palazzolo, Pro Football Focus’ former lead analyst, now with The 33rd Team. “Linemen generally take longer to develop. A switch can flip, and they can get it.

“And that is almost no money for a guy who is talented enough to go seventh overall. Even if you were to develop him into an average player at guard, it’s a massive win. So I get it from that perspective.”

Still, as Palazzolo reviewed and recited his notes on Neal from 2024 — the last time he played — he saw little to rave about, despite some run-blocking progress.

“Everything is unrefined with technique, with hands,” Palazzolo said. “He looks like a developmental five-star [college] freshman. The trajectory is in the right direction — but is it too late?”

Harbaugh doesn’t think so, and Palazzolo understands why the coach embraces that outlook, even at this stage of Neal’s career.

“It happens more at this position than any other,” Palazzolo said of the mid-career switch-flipping moment for an underwhelming offensive lineman. “But he still looked like he was completely unrefined the last time we saw him playing.”

Brown’s advice to Harbaugh: Get Neal comfortable by having him do more double-team blocking in the run game, instead of zone blocking, which could result in too many one-on-one situations for Neal. And simplify Neal’s pass blocking with more slide protection, so he blocks a predetermined area instead of a specific defensive lineman.

“That’s going to help build his confidence,” Brown said. “You don’t want to put him on the island a lot, because these guys revert back [to bad habits], and it throws them off.”

As the Giants prepare to begin their first offseason workouts under Harbaugh on Tuesday, Neal is running out of chances to leave his past mistakes behind.

Harbaugh isn’t worried about that. He loves how Neal still clings to “big dreams” for a successful NFL career.

With Neal getting one last shot to emerge from invisibility under a new coaching staff, Harbaugh has a bold message for him.

“Try to realize those dreams with us,” he said.

title: There's a key twist to Kyler Murray's Vikings contract
description: Minnesota likely made this compromise with the QB.
fullText:
There's a key twist to Kyler Murray's Vikings contract originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Kyler Murray's signing with the Minnesota Vikings has the potential to be one of the biggest moves of this NFL offseason if it works out.

The Vikings already had one of the most talented rosters in football, but they didn't have a quarterback they felt good about, not after J.J. McCarthy's rough 2025 season.

Now they've got a former No. 1 overall pick to play QB, a guy who has played a lot of good football in his NFL career.

Because the Arizona Cardinals are paying Murray a lot of money to not play for them this season, the Vikings even got him on a veteran's minimum contract.

There's just one thing about the contract that stands out, reported by ESPN's Dan Graziano in a new article this week: There's a clause that impacts who Murray plays for next season.

The contract Murray signed doesn't allow him to be given the franchise tag or transition tag next offseason, Graziano writes.

MORE: Eagles' new signing flew himself as a pilot to Philadelphia

That prevents the Vikings from keeping Murray without first letting him get to the open market.

"So, if Murray plays great football for the Vikings and they want to re-sign him, they could have competition from other teams," writes Graziano.

This obviously isn't something Minnesota would've wanted in the contract, so clearly Murray asked for it and the Vikings granted it. They wanted their QB for this season, and they likely feel they have the goods to convince Murray to re-sign if this year goes well.

Graziano compared Murray's contract to that of Tua Tagovailoa with the Atlanta Falcons -- Tua's deal doesn't have this equivalent cause.

Murray has the potential in high demand next offseason if he can put together a special campaign in Minnesota. If that happens, the Vikings will have to bid against everyone else.

title: SEC baseball pitcher power rankings: Do LSU's aces make the cut?
description: After three weeks of SEC play, here's our latest SEC baseball pitcher power rankings. Did William Schmidt make the top 10?
fullText:
The SEC baseball season is in full swing. April is here and three weekends of conference play are in the books. It's resume boosting time and the postseason will be here before we know it.

It takes a complete team to win the title and compete at the top of the SEC, but it starts with starting pitching. Specifically, starting pitching. An elite rotation can carry a team to a CWS title. Jay Johnson and LSU know that better than anyone.

There's another deep and talented group of SEC pitchers this year. MLB scouts will have their eyes on this conference down the stretch. We have the usual suspects, but we've seen breakout stars, too.

I'll take a shot at power ranking the top 10 starters in the SEC. These are power rankings -- not necessarily a reflection of the work they've done so far. Let's dive right in.

All of these guys are deserving of a spot in the top 10. Texas' Luke Harrison has a proven track record, but the K-BB% isn't at ace level. Casan Evans has the talent to be a top-five pitcher in the league, but we haven't seen him do it consistently yet.

UGA's Joey Volchko has strikeout stuff, but I want to see him find consistent command before bumping him into the top 10.

Schmidt is enjoying a breakout year for the Tigers. He was a prized recruit when he signed out of high school ahead of the 2025 season, but now that he's earned a spot in the rotation, he's putting it all together. Schmidt's 2.55 ERA ranks top 10 in the SEC, and his 25.9 K-BB% is No. 11.

Fay's 0.88 WHIP is elite, ranking No. 3 in the SEC. Earlier this year, Fay tossed a complete game shutout vs. a good Florida lineup. Fay struck out 11 in that contest.

Vigue was one of the SEC's most intriguing pitching additions of the transfer cycle and so far, he's delivered. Vigue earns a strikeout or induces a groundball 78.3% of the time, which ranks No. 2 in the SEC. The walk rate is over 12%, but Vigue only figures to get better as the season progresses.

Jelkin is rolling right now. After tossing seven solid innings vs. Ole Miss, he went eight innings vs. LSU and only allowed two runs. Few pitchers are doing what Jelkin is doing vs. SEC hitters right now. The right-hander owns a 2.81 ERA in 2026.

As I said, this is not a resume ranking of what a pitcher has done this year. Gaeckle's numbers aren't elite, but he's one of the top pitching prospects in the sport and the stuff is impossible to ignore. I'm not ready to move him down just yet.

Peterson is arguably the top pitching prospect in the SEC. His strikeout rate of 37.9% ranks No. 3 in the league and his ERA is 3.67. If Peterson can tamp down the walks and get a few more ground balls, he could finish the year No. 1 on this list.

Dietz's placement might be the most eye-opening on this list. His 4.19 ERA is a tick above the other guys in the top 10, but the underlying numbers are there. Dietz's K-BB% is among the best in the SEC. He can get hitters with a strong fastball, change-up, or curveball.

With an ERA of 0.91, Valincius leads all weekend starters in the SEC. He's only 5-foot-10, but don't let that fool you -- he has strikeout material. His K rate of 35.9% ranks No. 5 among qualified SEC pitchers.

Walks kill. So a good way to avoid dying is to avoid walking hitters. Marciano does just that with a 3.8% walk rate. I would trust Marciano in a critical postseason spot right now.

Texas has the best rotation in the SEC and it's led by Riojas. The K rate is elite at 41% and Riojas' 1.59 ERA ranks No. 4 among qualified SEC pitchers.

This article originally appeared on LSU Wire: SEC baseball pitcher power rankings: Do LSU's aces make the cut?

title: The Daily Mauling 4.1.26
description: Bill Self press conference announced for 1 pm.
fullText:
The Lady Jayhawks were knocked out of the WBIT in Semifinals against in conference rival BYU. The loss officially ends basketball season in Lawrence.

The fourth quarter was all about runs, starting with an 8-0 spurt by BYU as the Cougars took a 54-51 lead at the 6:48 mark. Meister ended the run with a three and Nichols added a pair of free throws, putting Kansas back on top, 56-54, just 68 seconds later. BYU hit three straight three-pointers as part of a 9-0 run, opening up a 63-56 advantage with 3:51 to play. BYU’s lead remained at nine into the final minute, when Brittany Harshaw knocked down a three-pointer and Davis scored to make it 69-65 with 15 seconds to play. Nichols added two more free throws to make it 70-67, which ended up as the final margin.

Over at SI, Joshua Schulman takes a look at the roster and makes some predictions on portal happenings:

Although KU has the ability to retool its roster with transfers across the country, there are some guys whom the program would certainly like to retain. But which players will end up leaving the university for more favorable options, and how many will there be?

Yesterday’s discussion talked about the need for a shooter on next year’s roster, is Jordan Ellerbee that guy? (video link)

Jayhawk Slant covers some of the new players that are looking to make a difference in the fall for Kansas football.

247 also has some updates on spring practice, this one focuses on the wide receivers and quarterbacks:

The first player that caught my attention on the first day of practice was MTSU transfer Nahzae Cox. Players were running to start drills when I saw him and I immediately thought “Who is that?” I spent more time watching him today and he has some impressive physical traits.

Through the Phog has some quotes from Andy Kotelnicki about what he’s seeing and looking for at spring practice as he prepares to put together his offense for the upcoming season:

“And really, what you look for in my practice, and being around the players, and seeing them and evaluating is so important, ’cause you’re looking for the ones who can consistently do it. Anybody can make a big time catching one-on-ones once. Can you do it day after day?

KCTV 5 also covers some of the things Kotelnicki had to say:

“He always checks to be like, ‘Are we on the same page about things?’ Do you know what I mean? He’s like, ‘Hey this is what’s gonna happen. I’m gonna have Isaiah (Marshall) come over here, and this is the reps he’s gonna take because he needs to come over and get these reps next,’” Kotelnicki said about Zebrowski. “He’s really really good, and excels at scheming, and thinking of other ways to do things.”

And the last Jayhawk links is from Through the Phog as they rank potential replacements in case Bill Self does decide to hang up the whistle.

Self has compiled one of the best coaching careers in the history of the sport, and whoever eventually fills his role will have massive shoes to fill. Our job today, is to try to scour the market for potential candidates who could fill said role.

This piece has been running through my mind since I read it a couple weeks ago and meant to link to it. Sam Miller took some time to write about things he learned from Russell Carleton and his process for writing and learning about baseball:

On August 7, Russell Carleton announced that he was semi-retiring from ball writing. Russell had been at Baseball Prospectus (with a couple interruptions to go work in the industry) since 2009. He wrote more than 500 columns there, some of which I got to edit; he wrote a great book called The Shift, which I got to blurb; he was an Effectively Wild guest or co-host at least 19 times; and, as I step back to think about it, I think he’s probably the writer who has had the most influence on my baseball knowledge. I want to share just a tiny portion of things I learned from Russell, things that will likely apply to ball for the rest of time whether he’s still writing or not.

Another story that I haven’t been able to get out of my brain the last couple days was written by Brandy Jensen and opens with this:

Too often these days I find myself in the position of defending someone I think is annoying from someone I know is dangerous.

We didn’t have a Defector link last week but we’ve got one today as Barry Petchesky writing on what fights can mean in hockey:

But rituals can matter for their own sake, and for the social cohesion they provide. The original philosophical principle of performativity referred not to insincerity trumping intent—virtue signaling, in other words—but to the use of words or acts that actually bring something into being. How being pronounced man and wife makes them married, for example. Or how fighting an opponent who injured your teammate might tangibly strengthen the bond of your team. Not because you hate that other guy or because you want him to feel physical pain as punishment, but because that’s what teammates are supposed to do.

QOTD: With Petchesky talking about how rituals can matter for their own sake, do you have any personal rituals that you use to keep everything balanced in life?

title: Dolphins GM explains why he traded WR Jaylen Waddle to the Broncos
description: The Dolphins sent Jaylen Waddle to the Broncos in a trade last month, but earlier this week was Jon-Eric Sullivan's first chance to address the move.
fullText:
The Miami Dolphins shocked the NFL world last month when they traded wide receiver Jaylen Waddle and a draft pick to the Denver Broncos for a bundle of three picks in the 2026 NFL draft, including one in the first round.

Waddle was one of the most productive receivers in the NFL during his five seasons with the Dolphins, catching 373 passes for 5,039 yards and 26 touchdowns despite spending most of that time as Miami's second option behind Tyreek Hill.

While the trade happened weeks ago, new Dolphins general manager Jon-Eric Sullivan hadn't publicly spoken about the deal, so he addressed the move earlier this week.

“I was not actively shopping. (Broncos GM George Paton) called. We had good conversations,” Sullivan said (transcribed by the South Florida Sun-Sentinel). “We talked over the period of a lot of days. What became clear to me was, this was the right thing to do for the Miami Dolphins. Waddle is 27 years old. We’re in an era where the market for receivers is through the roof.

“We’re in a position with our roster where we need to get younger and cheaper. It was in the best interest of the Miami Dolphins to get two rookie players, if I do my job right, if we do our job right as a personnel staff, that will be on rookie contracts that we can grow with, with Malik (Willis).”

While the return for the former first-round pick was strong, Miami needs to nail these picks because Waddle is in his prime and could've been a great weapon for Willis (or whoever's under center) for years to come.

More Dolphins: Dolphins address some of their biggest needs in new 7-round mock draft

This article originally appeared on Dolphins Wire: Dolphins GM explains why he traded WR Jaylen Waddle to the Broncos

title: Falcons hiring new assistant general manager
description: Falcons to hire Jeff Scott as assistant GM, per ESPN's Adam Schefter
fullText:
According to a report from ESPN's Adam Schefter, the Atlanta Falcons are adding Jeff Scott to their front office as the team's new assistant general manager. The former Eagles vice president of football operations spent the last five years working in Philadelphia.

"The Atlanta Falcons are set to hire Jeff Scott as their Assistant GM, per source," wrote Schefter. "Scott has worked across various departments within scouting and football operations, most recently with the Eagles. Scott also has been a participant in the NFL’s Accelerator Program."

Prior to his five-year stint with the Eagles (2021-2025). Scott spent 12 years working with Washington, starting as a salary cap intern in 2012. He worked his way up to be the team's director of pro scouting in 2020 before leaving to become a senior pro scout for the Ealges.

A native of Fredericksburg, VA, Scott graduated with a bachelor's degree in political science from Davidson College, where he played defensive back. During his sophomore year in 2005, he led the Pioneer Football League in fumbles forced. Scott received his master's degree in sports management from Georgetown University in 2012. - PhiladelphiaEagles.com

The Falcons hired Matt Ryan as the team's president of football and later added Ian Cunningham as general manager. Scott will work under Ryan and Cunningham in Atlanta's front office.

This article originally appeared on Falcons Wire: Atlanta Falcons to hire Jeff Scott as assistant GM

title: Patriots' QB plans revealed after cutting 9-year veteran
description: There is work to be done behind Drake Maye.
fullText:
Patriots' QB plans revealed after cutting 9-year veteran originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

The New England Patriots have their franchise quarterback, Drake Maye.

There's more to a QB depth chart than just one guy, though, and on that front, the Pats have some work to do.

Earlier this offseason, the Patriots cut Josh Dobbs, ending the nine-year veteran's tenure in New England.

They also made the move to sign Tommy DeVito to a two-year contract to remain with the Patriots.

At minimum, that appears to promote DeVito to the QB2 spot on the depth chart after he was in the third spot last season.

It also creates a potential need, though, and the Patriots plan to fill it.

"Maye enters his third NFL season, and now the team is planning to add a rookie No. 3 option behind him and Tommy DeVito, who signed a two-year, $7.4 million contract earlier this month," ESPN's Mike Reiss wrote in a new article on Tuesday.

MORE: Eagles' new signing flew himself as a pilot to Philadelphia

That adds some intrigue to the latter stages of the 2026 NFL Draft for Patriots fans.

Some picks are more exciting than others, and even while already having Maye in place, there's always something fascinating about picking a QB in the draft.

New England could always sign an undrafted free agent quarterback instead, but if there's someone on the board they like in the fifth, sixth or seventh round, don't be surprised if the Patriots snag him.

It'd be a cool spot for a young QB to arrive in, getting a chance to learn from Maye and potentially carve out an NFL career.

title: Kevin Porter Jr. is likely out for rest of NBA season with Bucks
description: A productive player for the Milwaukee Bucks, USC's Kevin Porter Jr., is expected to be out for the rest of the NBA season. Coach Doc Rivers commented
fullText:
Kevin Porter Jr. has authored a resurrection season this year for the Milwaukee Bucks. It has not been a great season for the Bucks, and a lot of the focus this season has been on whether Giannis Antetokounmpo was going to play for another team. During that time frame, Giannis has also missed a lot of time due to injury.

During that time, former USC Trojan Kevin Porter Jr. was the lead guy for the Milwaukee Bucks. Porter was on his way to having one of his best career seasons, and this year was full of great showings. This season, Porter Jr. was the second leading scorer for the Bucks, averaging 17.4 points per game, 7.4 assists per game, and shot 46.5% percent from the field.

It has been reported that head coach Doc Rivers does not believe Porter Jr. will see the court again this season. It is a tough blow, given what Porter was able to accomplish in just 38 games for the Bucks. He became a great facilitator and playmaker for Milwaukee, leading this offense with competent play without one of the biggest stars in the game.

Kevin Porter Jr. will be a free agent this upcoming offseason, but he has a player option, which will give him the decision on whether he will stay with the Bucks for another season. Given the success he had in his short sample size, staying in Milwaukee another year would be the best thing for his career. If there is a world without Giannis Antetokounmpo, the Bucks would be happy to retain Kevin Porter Jr. to be part of the new era.

This article originally appeared on Trojans Wire: Kevin Porter Jr. expected to miss rest of NBA season with Bucks

title: Terence Crawford Opens Up on Late Career Decision Before Retirement
description: Terence Crawford ended his boxing career at the top, but he came close to taking one more fight before stepping […]
fullText:
Terence Crawford ended his boxing career at the top, but he came close to taking one more fight before stepping away. The unbeaten star retired in late 2025 after defeating Canelo Álvarez by unanimous decision. That result pushed his record to 42-0 with 31 knockouts and made him a five-division world champion.

Terence Crawford moved up to 168 pounds for that bout and added super-middleweight titles to his résumé. His win over Canelo followed his dominant victory over Errol Spence Jr. in 2023, where he became undisputed at welterweight. With major titles across multiple weight classes, Crawford closed his career with a clean record and a long list of achievements that placed him among the sport’s top fighters.

In a recent interview, Terence Crawford said he had considered one more move before retiring. He thought about dropping to middleweight to chase another title. “I was like, well, go down to 160 and do it again,” he said. Crawford explained that he wanted to fight for something meaningful. “It was a thought,” he added. “To dare to be great and fight for something meaningful.”

He identified Janibek Alimkhanuly as the main target. The plan depended on a unification fight involving Alimkhanuly and Erislandy Lara. Crawford said the matchup would have drawn strong interest. “Janibek is a dangerous challenge,” he said. He also pointed to a bigger goal if things aligned. If Alimkhanuly had beaten Carlos Adames and become undisputed, it would have created a high-level clash. “Especially if he had somehow beaten Carlos Adames and [became the] undisputed [champion]. Then you [have a fight] undisputed vs undisputed.”

The plan did not move forward after Alimkhanuly tested positive for a banned substance. That result stopped the proposed unification bout and removed the path Crawford had in mind. He addressed the situation directly. “But Janibek popped,” Crawford said.

Without that fight, Terence Crawford decided not to continue. He viewed the outcome as a clear sign. “It didn’t happen. So, you know what, I was like this is God telling me you ain’t got nothing left to prove.” The 38-year-old chose not to pursue another title opportunity and confirmed his decision to retire after the Canelo fight.

Terence Crawford’s decision followed a period of major success. He became the undisputed champion at welterweight in 2023 after beating Spence. He then moved up two weight classes and defeated Canelo in 2025 to win the super middleweight titles. Across his career, he held lineal and undisputed titles and collected belts in five divisions.

A move to 160 pounds would have tested him again against a top-level opponent in Alimkhanuly. The middleweight champion is known for his pressure and power and has challenged several contenders. That fight did not happen, and Crawford chose to end his career without adding another bout.

He retired with an unbeaten record and multiple titles across different divisions. His final decision ensured that he left the sport without a loss and without returning for another fight.

title: International round-up: Thiago reacts to first Brazil goal after Croatia win
description: Igor Thiago's first Brazil goal was a decisive last-minute goal against Croatia on Tuesday night, as Carlo Ancelotti's side won 3-1 in their final game before World Cup squads are selected.The Brentfo...
fullText:
Danilo's first-half strike was cancelled out in the 84th minute by Lovro Majer, before Thiago's penalty and Gabriel Martinelli's late finish completed the late turnaround.

After the game, Thiago said: "It's the greatest achievement of my life. I thank God... God is faithful, God has already written my story, and this was the day he chose for me to represent my country and score my first goal with the Canarinha."

Ancelotti also stated: "The thing that pleases me the most is that the new players have made the most of their opportunities. Clearly, this makes the selection of the final squad more difficult. Because Igor Thiago, Léo Pereira, and Danilo played well. Endrick also played very well; Kaiki, too.”

Elsewhere, Mikkel Damsgaard and Mathias Jensen were beaten in their World Cup play-off final, as Denmark lost against Czech Republic on penalties. Damsgaard played 115 minutes before being replaced by Jensen, who put his penalty wide in the shoot-out.

Ethan Pinnock and Jamaica also missed their chance to go to the World Cup, losing in extra-time to DR Congo. Pinnock played 120 minutes in the 1-0 defeat.

In friendlies, Nathan Collins and Caoimhín Kelleher both featured for the Republic of Ireland against North Macedonia. Collins played 90 minutes, with Kelleher substituted on the hour mark, as the sides drew 0-0 in Dublin.

Ukraine also played on Tuesday night, winning 1-0 against Albania. Yehor Yarmoliuk played 64 minutes, picking up his ninth cap.

Elsewhere, Kristoffer Ajer started as Norway drew 0-0 with Switzerland; Hákon Valdimarsson started Iceland's 1-1 draw with Haiti; Kevin Schade was an unused substitute as Germany beat Ghana 2-1; Jordan Henderson wasn't in the England squad that lost 1-0 to Japan at Wembley.

Burkina Faso drew 1-1 with Guinea-Bissau, with Dango Ouattara starting the game, while Nigeria drew 2-2 with Jordan, as Frank Onyeka played 81 minutes for the Super Eagles.

Michael Kayode played 90 minutes as Italy Under-21s beat Sweden 4-0 in European Championship qualifying, which leaves them second in their group.

England Under-20s were held to a 3-3 draw by Italy as they travelled to Rome for a friendly, with Jayden Meghoma playing 78 minutes.

title: Mets Morning News: Shutout (No foolin’)
description: Your Wednesday morning dose of Mets and Major League Baseball news, notes, and links.
fullText:
The Mets suffered their second loss of the season, losing 3-0 to the Cardinals and being held to just six hits. Senga had a great start, giving up just two runs in the third inning and going six full innings with three walks and an impressive nine strikeouts. Richard Lovelady made another appearance, giving up a run in two innings while striking out two. But the damage was done the second the first Cardinals run scored, as the Mets were unable to scratch even one run across, marking their first shutout loss of the season.

Choose your recap: Amazin’ Avenue, MLB.com, Newsday, New York Daily News, New York Post

Joe Buck will be in the ESPN booth for the Jackie Robinson Day game between the Mets and Dodgers, and he’ll be joined by Orel Hershiser and Ron Darling.

ESPN compiled the six rookies making the biggest impact so far this season, including Mets rookie outfielder Carson Benge.

A medical emergency at Opening Day went from a scary situation to a feel-good outcome.

Jon Heyman gave some other options for the Mets should a Freddy Peralta extension not materialize.

J.D. Martinez has been added to the Mets front office as a special assistant to baseball operations.

Laura Albanese took a look at how J.D. Martinez’s return could help some other struggling Mets players.

The Mets and White Sox went through a few different permutations in the Luis Robert Jr. trade negotiations before landing on Luisangel Acuña.

Two former Mets in Jacob deGrom and Pete Alonso had some fun with each other during the game between the Rangers and Orioles in which Alonsa got his first non-Mets home run.

Andrew Painter pitched a gem in his highly-anticipated MLB debut, going 5.1 innings allowing just one run and striking out eight in a 3-2 Phillies win over the Nationals.

The Marlins continued their early season success, easily beating the White Sox 9-2, with Liam Hicks, Owen Caissie, and Griffin Conine driving in two runs apiece.

The Braves handed the Athletics their first win, losing 5-2 with pitcher José Suarez giving up four runs in 3.2 innings.

Top Mariners prospect Colt Emerson has agreed to an eight-year, $95 million extension that could reach a value of $130 million with all the escalators.

Colt Emerson’s extension was the largest-ever for a player yet to make his major league debut.

Buster Olney put together a group of polarizing teams and why you should—or shouldn’t—believe in them.

There may be another “Golden Generation” of shortstops coming to the major leagues.

Blue Jays pitcher Cody Ponce has an ACL sprain, but a return this season isn’t out of the realm of possibilities.

The MLBPA has amassed a war chest of $415 million ahead of a possible lockout.

Zach Eflin exited his season debut with the Orioles due to right elbow discomfort.

Carlos Rodón has hit a snag in his recovery from elbow surgery due to some hamstring tightness.

The Padres demolished the Mets in their first Opening Day meeting in 1997.

title: 9-9-9, closer entrances, and making Fetch happen
description: On MLB forcing it just a bit
fullText:
A couple things have induced dread in me in baseball’s first week. There have been major wins — the Yankee pitching staff is fantastic! ABS is a lot of fun! — but some cringey losses. The first is the corporatization of the 9-9-9 task, a Twitter meme that challenges fans to consume nine hot dogs and nine beers across the length of a nine-inning game. We at SB Nation cannot condone irresponsible drinking but it is a meme that’s not that hard to find across various social platforms.

Of course MLB teams can’t endorse irresponsible drinking either, and are in many jurisdictions legally capped on how many standard drinks they can sell at one time or in certain time frames. The logical endpoint to this is that teams can’t advertise that they want you to drink nine full-sized beers over the course of what may be a two and a half hour game, ergo, we get a cheap facsimile like what the San Francisco Giants had to offer Opening Weekend. Nine hot dogs that are better described as “hot dogs with slider characteristics” and a single 24-oz beer with nine flight glasses. They appear to be about 4 oz. glasses, although flights in general range from 2-6. Pretty simple math tells us that you’re out of your one allotted beer by the seventh inning — not even close to the spirit of the challenge.

In related news, we also got to see just how sauceless the baseball hegemon has become:

Now I don’t know who this young woman is, I’m sure she’s very nice and I’m damn sure she’s a better musician than me, but she is struggling and it didn’t get much better in Edwin Diaz’s second outing. This was followed up on Saturday with Mason Miller’s new entrance, which at this point just looks like everyone else’s:

Sparky Lyle was the first star reliever to really make an entrance An Entrance, just over 50 years ago. In 1963, in-stadium organists would play live to announce a pitching change, but in 1972 the 27-year old Lyle was gifted Pomp and Circumstances to accompany his Datsun-assisted arrival to the game, a gimmick that lightened up the darkness that were those early-70s Yankee squads.

A few closers since have had An Entrance, though my favorite part of Mariano Rivera’s was that the team asked him “hey Mo how do you feel about using this song” and he mostly shrugged and said yeah sure fine. But even in the Hall of Famer’s last ever appearance at the Stadium, he didn’t have the whole shindig about it:

I hope this doesn’t make me sound like a curmudgeon — then again maybe some curmudgeonly behavior would be useful right now — but I don’t think everyone needs a production, and if you’re going to get a production, make it good.

I do think the attempt to re-create the 9-9-9 and the push to give every single closer an entrance are related. We’re all in the content business, MLB included, we’re all trying to get short-form vertical video, we’re all trying to get segments on a podcast talking about the new menus this year. I don’t fault MLB for chasing those eyeballs, I just think you can’t commoditize them. One team having a designated home run celebration is fun and remarkable, all 30 having a designated prop turns things into an assembly line — hit home run, do celebration on camera. Call the bullpen, flicker the lights a bunch, repeat.

I actually have to give MLB a lot of credit over the past few seasons. It’s done a great job of highlighting what makes the game exciting — the era of Shohei Ohtani and Aaron Judge may lead to some oversaturation but when both have lived up to their billing it’s hard to get too grumpy about it. Ditto for Paul Skenes and Tarik Skubal. The World Baseball Classic drew more eyeballs than the NBA finals. Baseball is on a rise without the need to push the gimmicks.

There are always going to be spontaneous moments that bring in more joy to baseball. The Mets and Gay Grimace were a perfect example, or when Ronald Torreyes pretended to be a late night host almost a decade ago. Handing out a toy video camera to the first 20,000 fans on one Thursday night would have been a great idea. Handing them out every single night kills the gimmick. Bringing memes from the offline world into the online carries more risk than I’m confident MLB PR teams can handle, and we all need to be more judicious about just who deserves flashing lights in the ninth.

title: Jersey Bulls boosted as Springfield gets league approval
description: Jersey Bulls will continue to be able to play at Isthmian League level after Springfield Stadium meets the criteria for the division.
fullText:
Jersey Bulls will continue to be able to play at Isthmian League level after Springfield Stadium met the criteria for the division.

The Bulls had until 31 March to make upgrades to the ground to bring it up to the required standards for the fourth tier of non-league football in England.

Had the Bulls, who won promotion last season, failed to get approval they would have been barred from this season's play-offs and could have faced relegation back to the Combined Counties League.

Among the work done at the ground in conjunction Jersey's government has been improvements to toilet facilities and new turnstiles.

There have also been improvements to its facilities for the media and the installation of new fencing around the ground.

The islanders are in their first season in the division and are fourth in the table as they look to book a play-off place.

title: Umpire C.B. Bucknor in spotlight again over blatant missed call
description: Three days after having six pitches overturned, umpire C.B. Bucknor had another call reversed by replay that had managers in both dugouts chuckling.
fullText:
Three days after having six of his calls overturned by MLB's new Automated Balls and Strikes system, umpire C.B. Bucknor was in the spotlight once again Tuesday night.

Serving as the first-base umpire in Milwaukee for the Brewers' March 31 game against the Tampa Bay Rays, Bucknor had one of the most blatant blown calls you'll ever see overturned by replay, leading to similar amused reactions in both dugouts.

The play occurred in the sixth inning with Milwaukee leading 4-2 and first baseman Jake Bauers at the plate. Bauers' line drive went off the glove of Rays second baseman Ben Williamson, who then threw wildly to first base.

The Rays retrieved the ball and tagged Bauers, whom Bucknor called out for failing to touch first base.

CB Bucknor strikes again, this time saying the runner did not touch first base. He very clearly did pic.twitter.com/zn6k9RtLXl

— Jomboy Media (@JomboyMedia) April 1, 2026

As the television announcers pointed out, Bucknor didn't even seem to be looking at Bauers' foot as he clearly stepped on the first base bag.

It didn't take long for replay to overturn the call.

Bauers ended up stealing second base and coming around to score on Brandon Lockridge's RBI double as the Brewers went on to a 6-2 win.

"I don't know what happened," Bauers told reporters after the game, avoiding repeated opportunities to comment any further. "I'm just thankful to get on base and thankful to come around and score."

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: C.B. Bucknor has blatant missed call reversed in Brewers-Rays game

title: Lyon’s Afonso Moreira ‘could have cried’ upon his return from injury
description: Having joined Olympique Lyonnais as a promising young player last summer, Afonso Moreira (21) has quickly established himself as one of the team’s key players. The second most decisive player in Pau...
fullText:
Having joined Olympique Lyonnais as a promising young player last summer, Afonso Moreira (21) has quickly established himself as one of the team’s key players. The second most decisive player in Paulo Fonseca’s side this season (six goals and nine assists in 30 appearances), the Portuguese player has stepped up perfectly in the absence of Malick Fofana (20), who suffered a serious injury to his right ankle last October.

However, it hasn’t all been plain sailing for the Sporting CP-trained forward, whose own season was cut short for several weeks after he picked up a hamstring injury in mid-February. Fit to return to the pitch for the Europa League round of 16 second leg against Celta Vigo (0-2), Moreira received an uncommonly warm welcome from his supporters.

That was a special moment that left a deep impression on the 21-year-old. “Right from the warm-up, we could feel the atmosphere; the crowd started chanting our names. If I’d been out of sight, I might have cried – it’s so overwhelming to receive that kind of love from the fans,” he admitted in an interview with Le Progrès. “I’m really passionate about football and this club, and I think they can sense that – that’s probably why we have such a good connection.”

title: Chase DeLauter And 2 Other Fantasy Baseball Sell-High Candidates After Week 1
description: Here are three sell-high candidates for fantasy baseball managers to consider moving early in the MLB season.
fullText:
We're a week into the MLB regular season and players have already set themselves apart from a production standpoint. Fantasy managers could capitalize on their value and shop potential returns in the trade market. Selling high is an optimal route for managers to acquire valuable assets or address positions of need through trades. Sell-high candidates include players flashing upward trends of production who may not sustain their elevated volume throughout the entirety of the season. Let's look at three sell-high candidates for fantasy baseball managers to consider early in the regular season:

Cleveland Guardians rookie Chase DeLauter is off to arguably the hottest start of any player in the American League this season. DeLauter has suited up in each of Cleveland's first six games, posting five runs, six hits, four home runs and five RBIs. He's sustaining encouraging efficiency with a .273 batting average and a .818 SLG. The rookie will garner significant trade interest in fantasy and could be moved for a reliable veteran asset. It's hard to project DeLauter sustaining such high-end production for the entirety of his first major league season, though his output to this point of the season is worth noting. The former first-round pick suffered an apparent foot injury during Wednesday night's matchup against the Los Angeles Dodgers and should be monitored in fantasy over the immediate future.

The New York Mets made an aggressive move ahead of the season, trading for Luis Robert Jr. in a deal with the Chicago White Sox. Robert is off to an encouraging start to the 2026 campaign, though questions remain regarding the sustainability of his fantasy output. Through five games, the 2023 All-Star is hitting .333 from the plate, racking up three runs, five hits, a homer and five RBIs with a 1.007 OPS. Robert has struggled to recapture his 2023 production over recent years, a trend that could persist in 2026 despite his hot start. His fantasy stock is climbing and his trade value is high early in the season. Considering his string of injuries and inconsistent play over the past few seasons, managers should keep an open mind on a potential fantasy trade involving Robert. For his asking price, managers could find greater consistency and sustained production in a potential return.

Luke Raley has emerged as a pleasant surprise for the Seattle Mariners to start the 2026 season. He's already nearly eclipsed his home run mark for the entire 2025 campaign and is rapidly bolstering his fantasy stock. Over four games this season, Raley is hitting .286 with three runs, four hits, three homers and six RBIs. The 31-year-old showed out during Spring Training and has carried such momentum into the regular season on the way to a remarkable start to the year. Raley won't possess the same value as Robert or DeLauter in the trade market among fantasy leagues, but could still garner a significant haul considering his ADP coming into the season. Managers could move Raley for a more proven asset while his trade value remains elevated.

title: Florida Panthers New President Promises to Put Fans First
description: SUNRISE — The Florida Panthers introduced their new president of business of operations on Tuesday with Mike White saying his No. 1 priority is the fan. White, who comes to the Panthers from the Amazon-owned robotaxi company Zoox, spent 11 years as an executive at Disney working both with the parks and with ESPN. “There […] This article: Florida Panthers New President Promises to Put Fans First originally appeared on Florida Hockey Now.
fullText:
SUNRISE — The Florida Panthers introduced their new president of business of operations on Tuesday with Mike White saying his No. 1 priority is the fan.

White, who comes to the Panthers from the Amazon-owned robotaxi company Zoox, spent 11 years as an executive at Disney working both with the parks and with ESPN.

“There are a lot of things we want to bring to the fans here,’’ White said. “But how do we amplify some of those things? … There’s really nothing like the guest experience at Disney, but here at the Panthers, we’re going to create really extraordinary fan experiences.’’

White replaces Matt Caldwell who left the Panthers after their second consecutive Stanley Cup championship last summer to become the CEO of the Minnesota Timberwolves and Lynx.

White will be in charge of all of the Panthers’ business entities including the team, arena, IcePlex, IceDen, and the War Memorial.

He said at his introductory press conference Tuesday at Amerant Bank Arena that he was “humbled to be here,’’ and promised to be a “steward for the brand, the team, the community, and, especially the fans.’’

White was introduced by two people he spent a lot of time talking to over the past couple of months in owner Vinnie Viola and general manager Bill Zito.

“Mike comes from a very accomplished career as a visionary,” Viola said. “He’s a creative, innovative leader. … The whole purpose of this organization is to respect our fans and give them the absolute best experience in sports both on and off the ice.’’

Added Zito: “I am excited to for what can be and for what we can do together to help. I am literally talking about hockey and the impact that Mike and his experience, his intellect, and his character will have on us. Not to mention the myriad of things that you can do business-wise for our franchise in general. ”

Although White will be working mostly on the business side of the organization, it was key he and the hockey operations department gets along as well as they did with Caldwell.

White says he and Zito hit it off right away.

That was pretty evident on Tuesday.

“Meeting Bill, it just felt natural,’’ White said. “It was one of those things where you were just like, ‘yeah, this is where I need to be’. I love Amazon. I had a great job and had no intention of leaving until they came into my life.’’

FHN: Florida Panthers Go Dark on Comcast Xfinity during Tuesday’s Game

A graduate of Ohio State who said he returns to Columbus at least once a year for a Buckeyes football game, White says he is passionate about sports.

“Obviously very aware of what they’re doing,’’ White said of the Panthers. “This is a world-class organization that has won two years in a row.’’

Where: Amerant Bank Arena, Sunrise

Local TV: Scripps Sports — WSFL 39 (Miami/FTL); WHDT 9 (WPB); LAFF 36.3 (SWFla)

Radio: WQAM; WBZT 1230-AM (Palm Beach); WCTH 100.3-FM (Florida Keys); SiriusXM

Panthers Radio Streaming: SiriusXM 932, NHL App

This Season (Panthers Lead 2-0) — At Florida: Panthers 5, Bruins 4 (Feb. 4); Thursday. At Boston: Panthers 4, Bruins 3 (Oct. 21)

All-time Regular Season Series: Boston leads 66-41-7, 6 ties

All-Time Postseason Series (Panthers Lead 2-0): Florida won 2023 First Round (4-3); Florida won 2024 ECSF (4-2).

Up Next for the Panthers: Saturday at Pittsburgh Penguins, 5 p.m.

This article: Florida Panthers New President Promises to Put Fans First originally appeared on Florida Hockey Now.

title: Which Gunners Are In Contention For The World Cup ?
description: Gunners will arrive back at different times this week from various places around the world in contrasting moods.Noorgard and Calafiori might mentally need a rest after their experiences on Tuesday nig...
fullText:
Gunners will arrive back at different times this week from various places around the world in contrasting moods.

Noorgard and Calafiori might mentally need a rest after their experiences on Tuesday night. Thank goodness they are not living in Denmark and Italy, where they would have to face the backlash.

Alternatively, in the space of a week, Viktor Gyökeres scored 4 times to take Sweden to the World Cup.

Thomas Tuchel called up 37 names in the last fortnight for an unofficial audition for a ticket to North America. Rice and Saka will be on the plane, but Eze, Maduake and White might need strong ends to the season.

While it will never be admitted, Gabriel and Saliba were rested for the international break because they know their place is safe in the Brazil and France squad.

Martinelli faces a lot of competition in the Brazilian attack, while Jesus was not selected for the last batch of fixtures but has been training back in South America to prove his fitness to Carlo Ancelotti.

After a couple of long-term injuries, health would be the only reason Kai Havertz does not represent Germany this summer.

Odegaard will captain Norway at their first tournament in 28 years as long as there are no further issues with his knee.

Hincapie was part of an Ecuador team that finished runner-up in the South American table, although he plays mostly as a centre-back for his country.

The Netherlands use Timber anywhere in a back 3 or 4 due to his versatility and because they are spoilt for options at right-back.

Zubimendi and Raya might start on the Spanish bench in the US, Canada and Mexico.

Mosquera last month turned down Colombia to play for Spain. It is unlikely Merino plays for Arsenal again this season after surgery on a fractured bone in his right foot.

Although it remains debated in Belgium what his best position is, Trossard has been part of the setup under Rudi Garcia in qualifying.

We will know by the 30th May how many Gunners for certain will be at the World Cup.

title: Cleveland Browns NFL Draft Profile: QB Taylen Green, Arkansas
description: HC Todd Monken has experience with dual-threat QBs. But does Green bring enough to the table to be worth the effort?
fullText:
If you were to play a word association game with even the most casual NFL fan, the response to the phrase “Cleveland Browns” is almost guaranteed to be, “needs a quarterback.”

Such is life when you are a franchise that has cycled through 42 different starting quarterbacks since 1999. Young, old, promising, washed-up, emergency starters, and starters who would rather spend practice week in Las Vegas. Name the scenario, and odds are there is a Browns quarterback to fill the square.

Cleveland is back at it again this year, as there is a growing movement to accept that Deshaun Watson, who has not been good since 2020 and has only played 19 games in the past five years, is the best quarterback currently on the roster. (Let’s take a break to allow the readers to let that sink in for a moment.)

Because the Browns find themselves in a familiar situation, just about every eligible quarterback in the 2026 NFL Draft will be, or has been, linked to the team in a mock draft.

There is one quarterback, given head coach Todd Monken’s past success, who may be on the team’s radar, however, and that is Taylen Green of Arkansas.

Taylen Green’s physical tools are BEYOND ELITE, but he hasn’t really improved at using them in four seasons as a starter. 

While his mechanics have improved, his overall accuracy, decision making, and pocket manipulation haven’t. 

Truly a mixed bag: pic.twitter.com/Wevi8ECf61

— NFL Draft Files (@NFL_DF) March 3, 2026

Height/Weight: 6-foot-6, 227 pounds

2025 Passing Stats: 12 games, 60.7 completion percentage, 2,714 passing yards, 19 passing touchdowns, 11 interceptions, 143.2 quarterback rating

2025 Rushing Stats: 12 games, 777 rushing yards, 5.6 yards per rush, 64.8 rushing yards per game, 8 rushing touchdowns.

Career Passing Stats: 53 games, 60.1 completion percentage, 9,662 passing yards, 59 passing touchdowns, 35 interceptions, 138.8 quarterback rating

Career Rushing Stats: 53 games, 2,405 rushing yards, 5.2 yards per rush, 45.4 rushing yards per game, 35 rushing touchdowns.

Average “Big Board” Position as of Publishing Date from Mock Draft Database: 149th overall, projected fifth round

The Draft Network’s Grade/Round Value: Round 4 – Developmental Traits

Taylen Green is a QB prospect in the 2026 draft class. He scored a 9.99 RAS out of a possible 10.00. This ranked 2 out of 1067 QB from 1987 to 2026.https://t.co/hJQbrJcfaX pic.twitter.com/R4n1teETUm

— RAS.football (@MathBomb) March 18, 2026

A long, rangy, dual-threat quarterback with upside, Green’s ability to generate explosive plays as a runner and passer helped keep his offense afloat. In-game consistency has been a sticking point, though. He has a long, unorthodox delivery and tends to baby too many throws. He can generate enough velocity and distance to stress defenses over the second and third levels. Green puts the ball in harm’s way at an alarming rate, which will translate to greater trouble against more talented defenders and more complicated coverage reads. His ability to win with his legs on called runs or pocket breaks pushes his value beyond his passing profile. Green’s upside will be tantalizing, but his ceiling will only track with his growth as a passer.

What an Expert is Saying (Bonus Round):

(Green’s) size will be alluring to general managers and head coaches. He is a legit athlete at just under 6-foot-6 and 225 pounds who rushed for more than 2,400 yards in his four seasons as a starter. He is a good rushing threat with experience in the pistol and shotgun as an RPO quarterback. As a passer, his athletic gifts show up in a naturally strong arm that can push the ball vertically.

However, he is still far too inconsistent as a passer. His footwork is OK, but it can be erratic. Pair that with a longer, sidearm throwing motion, and you have a faulty foundation for accuracy, evidenced by lower accuracy and ball-placement scores. He also doesn’t have the passing numbers you’d expect under pressure or on the run. Despite being a good athlete, he has a high sack rate, in part due to an average time to throw well above 3.00 seconds.

One of the reasons that Green will be linked to the Browns is because of the success that Monken had with quarterback Lamar Jackson in Baltimore. Green, like Jackson, is a dual-threat who can tuck the ball in and take off when needed.

That all sounds good, except for the reality that Green is not on the same level as Jackson. There is no shame in that, Jackson is a two-time league MVP after all (even if others played deserved it more). So while Jackson is the focal point of Baltimore’s offense, Green is projected to be a development player who, if all goes well, could see the field for a handful of plays in any given game.

It is not unreasonable to believe that Monken could get the most out of Green, but with the Browns needing to focus all their attention on finding an actual starting quarterback, even using a late-round selection on Green feels like a luxury the team can’t afford.

Browns Player Drafting Could Impact

If the Browns draft a quarterback, then Dillon Gabriel is likely the odd man out. (He may be anyway.) If the Browns draft a good quarterback, then Shedeur Sanders could be taking his show somewhere else this fall.

Priority: The red light is flashing away non-stop.

What are your thoughts on Taylen Green? Should the Browns take a gamble on him? Let us know in the comments!

title: Bombs away: Big hits propel Hartselle over Athens in ranked matchup
description: Apr. 1—HARTSELLE — A week after being named the No. 1 softball team in Class 6A, the Hartselle Tigers certainly looked the part. In a game that featured six lead changes, Hartselle took control late, scoring 11 runs in the fourth and fifth innings to run-rule No. 6 Athens 17-7.
fullText:
Apr. 1—HARTSELLE — A week after being named the No. 1 softball team in Class 6A, the Hartselle Tigers certainly looked the part.

In a game that featured six lead changes, Hartselle took control late, scoring 11 runs in the fourth and fifth innings to run-rule No. 6 Athens 17-7.

"It's exciting because they're a really good team and you have to play your best to beat them," Hartselle head coach Anna Hanners said. "Our team was disciplined at the plate today, and it paid off big time."

Hartselle has been explosive at the plate all season, with so many total home runs that Hanners wasn't sure how many the team had. Tuesday was more of the same as the Tigers blasted three including two from RC Wolf, who finished with a grand slam and seven RBIs.

"Honestly I was just thinking base hit every time I was up (at the plate)," Wolf said. "I just wanted to work the count, keep my head down and drive it, and they ended up going over the fence."

Katie Norgard added the other home run and finished with two hits and three RBIs. Abby Lopez led Athens with three hits, including a home run, and four RBIs

With several players that have hit multiple home runs this season, it's become a race among them to see who can hit the most. Norgard leads the team currently, but with two more on Tuesday, Wolf is in the thick of the race.

"This team has something special to it. There's a lot of talent, and we all have been hitting the ball well," Wolf said. "That's all that matters at the end of the day, but I will say I think I have a good chance" to hit the most home runs.

On the other side, Athens head coach Chuck Smith said his team will shake off the loss, but that they also need to change their approach.

"We can't decide when we play a great team like Hartselle that our focus is going to be higher," he said. "We have to attack every game the same no matter if we're playing Hartselle, Saraland in the state championship or a rec-league team."

With the win, Hartselle is in the driver's seat of Class 6A, Area 15. However, Hanners said the team won't view it that way.

"I don't want to think like that because anything can happen. We have to continue to improve and play our best," she said.

— caleb.suggs@decaturdaily.com or 256-340-2395. Twitter @CalebSuggs2

title: USC Big Ten ranking in one preseason prediction model unveiled
description: The new offseason SP+ rankings are out. See where USC landed in this first preseason measurement of the Big Ten's 18 programs:
fullText:
USC football faces a lot of uncertainties heading into the 2026 college football season and the Big Ten schedule. The Trojans have a tough road this season, playing Washington, Oregon, Penn State, Indiana, and Ohio State. USC will need to win three of those five tough games and then win all their other contests to achieve a 10-2 record, which is the consensus on what USC will need to make the College Football Playoff. 9-3 is unlikely to get the job done with the playoff still at 12 teams and not at 16. Preseason ratings offer a first look at where the analytics have USC and other Big Ten teams. Wolverines Wire has more:

"The doldrums of the offseason are disappearing, and one of the major signs of such is the revelation of various rankings -- beyond the ones that come out the day after the national championship game. The one that you can probably put the most stock in is that of the SP+, compiled by ESPN advanced stats guru Bill Connelly. SP+ is a predictive model, one that balances returning production, recent records, recruiting, and coaching changes. In the preseason SP+, the Big Ten is quite top-heavy, with several teams in the top 25."

The Wolverines Wire article notes that USC is No. 4 in the Big Ten in the SP+ preseason rankings:

"Lincoln Riley changed a lot about the program after a better, but still disappointing season. Will his coaching staff changes get the Trojans back to the top? Seems unlikely, but the offense should still be very, very good."

The offense might need to be great, not just very good, for the Trojans to get to 10-2. We will see what happens.

This article originally appeared on Trojans Wire: Bill Connelly's SP+ offers early preseason ranking of USC in Big Ten

title: Prep Roundup: Cross pitches shutout for Lawrence County
description: Apr. 1—KILLEN — Bella Cross was a force at the plate and in the circle for Lawrence County on Tuesday, homering twice and pitching five shutout innings as the Red Devils defeated Brooks 12-0 in high school softball. Cross finished 3-for-3 at the plate with four RBIs, while allowing just two hits and one walk while striking out two to earn the win in the circle. Alivia Terry had three hits and ...
fullText:
Apr. 1—KILLEN — Bella Cross was a force at the plate and in the circle for Lawrence County on Tuesday, homering twice and pitching five shutout innings as the Red Devils defeated Brooks 12-0 in high school softball.

Cross finished 3-for-3 at the plate with four RBIs, while allowing just two hits and one walk while striking out two to earn the win in the circle.

Alivia Terry had three hits and two RBIs for Lawrence County, while Reagan Smith and Maddie Graham added two hits and one RBI each.

Clements 22, Tanner 2: Olivia Moody went 4-for-4 with a home run and four RBIs as Clements rolled past Tanner on Tuesday.

Hayslee DuBose had four hits and three RBIs for the Colts, while Zailey Moran added three hits, including a pair of triples, and four RBIs.

Lindsay Lane 10, Lexington 2: Charleigh Reding had two hits and two RBIs for Lindsay Lane on Tuesday.

Madison Lee had a double and two RBIs for the Lions, while Reding pitched all seven innings for the win, striking out six.

Hewitt-Trussville 9, Austin 5: Grace Lamb doubled and drove in a pair of runs for Austin on Tuesday. Liann Sanchez had three singles and one RBI for the Black Bears.

Muscle Shoals 12, Hatton 7: Prentiss Fowler led Hatton with three hits and three RBIs on Tuesday.

Catherine Willis homered, doubled and drove in two runs for the Hornets, while Adilynn Petersen added two hits, including a double, and two RBIs.

Hazel Green 9, Ardmore 8: Raelynn Dempsey tripled and drove in three runs for Ardmore on Tuesday.

Kiki Embry had two hits and two RBIs for the Tigers, while Piper Abbott added a hit and two RBIs. Fallon Broadway singled three times and drove in a run.

Austin 6, East Limestone 3: Riley Grigg singled twice and drove in a pair of runs for Austin on Tuesday.

Levi Morgan homered and drove in two runs for the Black Bears, while Trey Harville tripled, doubled and drove in a run. Owen Prater pitched 5 2/3 innings for the win, allowing two unearned runs on four hits with six strikeouts.

Brodie Coker had two RBIs for East Limestone.

Hartselle 5, Muscle Shoals 4: Asher Doepel, Cruise Baker and Cole Miles had one RBI each for Hartselle on Tuesday.

Brody Weathers had a pair of doubles for the Tigers, while Cain Falciani added two singles. Barrett McKelvey pitched 4 2/3 innings for the win, striking out five.

Athens 13, Decatur 2: Eli Roberts had a pair of hits, including a double, and four RBIs in a big win for Athens on Tuesday.

Bishop Morton had three hits and three RBIs for the Golden Eagles, while Brady Johnston added two hits, including a double, and three RBIs. John Lawrence Chittam had four hits and one RBI and Hudson Marks pitched all five innings for the win, striking out six.

Harris Penley had two hits and one RBI for Decatur, while Brody Holloway added a single and one RBI.

Lindsay Lane 9, East Lawrence 1: Cooper Abernathy had three hits, including a double, and three RBIs to lead Lindsay Lane on Tuesday.

Wyatt Wallace homered, singled and drove in three runs for the Lions and Grayson Miller pitched three innings for the win, striking out three.

Jaxon Parker drove in a run for East Lawrence, while Reece Hamilton added two hits, including a double.

Athens Bible School 14-16, Meek 0-8: Luke Murrell tossed a five-inning no-hitter and went 3-for-3 with a double and four RBIs in the first game of a doubleheader for Athens Bible on Tuesday.

Murrell allowed just one walk and struck out 11 while throwing 48 of 86 pitches for strikes. Ethan Barnes had two hits, including a triple, and four RBIs for the Trojans and Jake Banta had a pair of singles and two RBIs.

Micah Gwin homered, singled and drove in six runs to lead the Trojans in game two. Murrell went 3-for-4 with a triple, double and one RBI and Colton Hayes added two hits and two RBIs.

Clements 14, Danville 5: Grayson Craig had two hits, including a double, and four RBIs for Clements on Tuesday.

Cannon Lovell homered, singled and drove in three runs for the Colts, while Grayson Borden added three hits, including a triple, and two RBIs. Craig pitched 4 1/3 innings for the win, striking out seven.

Brayden Bass led Danville with a hit and two RBIs.

Elkmont 31, Asbury 2: Isaac Barnett led Elkmont with five RBIs in a blowout win on Tuesday.

Bentley Mitchell drove in four runs for the Red Devils, while Andrew Staggs, Braidyn Amick and Ethan Dunlap added three RBIs each. Layton Schrimsher pitched four innings for the win, striking out 10.

Randolph 7, West Limestone 5: Jasper Griffin doubled and drove in two runs for West Limestone on Tuesday.

Colten Lewter had two hits and one RBI for the Wildcats, who were outhit 10-5 in the loss.

Foley 6, Brewer 3: Hunter Knighten and Dane Homan had two hits and one RBI each for Brewer on Tuesday.

Decatur earns section sweep: Sheridan South (6-1, 6-0), Evelyn Burnett (6-0, 6-1), Lydia South (6-1, 6-1), Emory Leffers (6-1, 6-0), Katherine Botto (6-0, 6-0) and Annie Pylant (6-1, 6-1) each picked up singles wins as the Decatur girls swept Mortimer Jordan in section play on Monday.

Doubles pairs of South-South (6-0, 6-2), Burnett-Pylant (6-0, 6-1) and Leffers-Botto (6-0, 6-0) earned points to close out the sweep.

title: Matayo Uiagalelei looking to become better finisher in final year with Ducks
description: Oregon's Matayo Uiagalelei and Dan Lanning have identified an area for the senior pass rusher to improve in his final season with the Ducks.
fullText:
A stinging loss to end your season in the College Football Playoff can lead to lots of free time to think about what went wrong and what you could have done to be better.

For some, that might be a negative spiral. But, for Matayo Uiagalelei, it was ample time for him to both decide to return to the Oregon Ducks for his final season and diagnose what he needed to improve upon to get the Ducks over the hump in the postseason.

Uiagalelei made 34 tackles, 9.5 for a loss, and six sacks last season. He also forced two fumbles. It was another impressive season for Uiagalelei, but his numbers were down from his 10-sack performance in 2024. Teitum Tuioti led the Ducks with 9.5 sacks in 2025, but Uiagalelei was still impactful in the backfield. Most notably, he stripped Texas Tech quarterback Behren Morton in the Orange Bowl and nearly returned the ball for a touchdown.

That was a physical play in which Uiagalelei tore through the right tackle and bullied Morton in the backfield before shaking a tackle on the return. It was one huge play in Oregon's season, but that's what he and coach Dan Lanning want to see more of this fall.

“Just keep getting bigger, faster, stronger, and then just finishing plays on the football side," Uiagalelei said. "Being more physical in the run, and just a lot of plays that I left on the table, like missed tackles, and a lot of opportunities I kind of took advantage of, and I just didn't make a play.”

Uiagalelei said he wants to be around 265 pounds with more muscle once the season rolls around. The 6-foot-5 senior is a leader for Oregon's defense and has been a dominant edge rusher since he arrived in Eugene, but Lanning thinks he can take his game to the next level by being stout against the run as well. With some young and uncertain depth along the defensive line after a series of transfers from last season's unit, the new rotation up front could lead to Uiagalelei playing every down and needing to make a bigger impact against the run.

"That's a place that I know he wants to attack and improve," Lanning said. "He's always been a really intelligent player. Obviously has a special skill set, and he's done a lot of things in the weight room this offseason to attack places for him to get better."

Similar to how quarterback Dante Moore has taken on a more vocal leadership role this spring, the same could be said for Uiagalelei.

"I think he has that voice," Lanning said. "His ability to continue to share that voice with the team is gonna be something that can impact us."

As a senior with plenty of big-game experience, it will be up to him and Tuioti to establish the standard for younger edge rushers like Nasir Wyatt, Anthony "Tank" Jones and Elijah Rushing, all of whom are next in line to be star pass rushers for the Ducks in the future.

Contact/Follow @Ducks_Wire on X (formerly Twitter) and like our page on Facebook to follow ongoing coverage of Oregon Ducks news, notes, and opinions.

This article originally appeared on Ducks Wire: Matayo Uiagalelei hoping for big improvement in final season at Oregon

title: Projected win totals for Broncos and their opponents in 2026
description: The Broncos will face a tough schedule in 2026. Here's a look at the projected win totals for the team's upcoming opponents.
fullText:
After going 14-3 last season and clinching the No. 1 seed in the AFC playoffs, the Denver Broncos have been given an over/under of 9.5 wins in 2026, according to odds from BetMGM.

To hit the over this fall, the Broncos would need to win 10 or more games (which they did in 2024 and 2025). Looking at the team's list of opponents, Denver's going to face a tough schedule in 2026.

Six of the team's upcoming opponents have an over/under of 10 wins or more, and three others are at 8 or higher. Oddsmakers consider just four of the Broncos' opponents to be "easy" matchups next season with five or fewer projected wins.

Based on 2025 records, Denver's strength of schedule (.512) ranks 15th in the league. The NFL's finalized 2026 schedule with dates and times is expected to be announced in May.

title: 'I think Hurzeler's going nowhere'
description: Former Brighton forward Warren Aspinall says Fabian Hurzeler is "very happy" at the club amid links with a move away in the summer.  Hurzeler has been linked with a return to Germany in the Bundesliga, with Bayer Leverkusen reportedly a possible destination.  "There's a lot of talk about Hurzeler but I think he's going nowhere," Aspinall told the Albion Unlimited podcast.
fullText:
Former Brighton forward Warren Aspinall says Fabian Hurzeler is "very happy" at the club amid links with a move away in the summer.

Hurzeler has been linked with a return to Germany in the Bundesliga, with Bayer Leverkusen reportedly a possible destination.

"There's a lot of talk about Hurzeler but I think he's going nowhere," Aspinall told the Albion Unlimited podcast.

"He's in the best league in the world.

"Yes he's German, but what is he going to do with Bayer Leverkusen? All the spotlight is on the Premier League. If you're doing well there you get a chance to go to a big big club.

"Leverkusen would be a sideways step. He's getting his way of football at Brighton. He's had 18 months and he's doing OK I think.

"He wants to stay, it's just paper talk. Nobody wanted him when we were on that poor run and there were calls for him to leave Brighton. Now we've won a few suddenly the papers talk about him leaving again.

"He did a Q&A the other day in London with Paul Barber. I think he's very happy at Brighton and the only way he can get into Europe right now is being successful in these last seven games."
